session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_title: From Persistence to Relapse: Evolution of Drug Resistance in Lung Cancer

speaker.name: Aaron Hata
speaker.affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital and Harvard Medical School

targets.dependency_context: EGFR mutant NSCLC after third‑gen TKI
EGFR mutant NSCLC with acquired BRAF mutation

models.name: EGFR mutant lung cancer cell lines
ALK mutant lung cancer cell lines
PDX models of ALK‑positive lung cancer

MOA_summary: Hata demonstrated that drug‑tolerant persister cells in EGFR‑ or ALK‑mutant NSCLC up‑regulate APOBEC3A, leading to increased APOBEC signature mutagenesis, DNA damage, and acquisition of resistance mutations such as EGFR T790M, C797S, or ALK E1210K. Knock‑down of APOBEC3A suppresses mutagenesis and prevents emergence of resistant clones in vitro and in xenografts, implicating therapy‑induced APOBEC activity as a driver of multi‑step resistance evolution.
key_findings: Drug‑tolerant persister NSCLC cells up‑regulate APOBEC3A within 1‑2 days of targeted therapy exposure.
APOBEC3A induction correlates with increased APOBEC signature (SBS2/13) mutations and structural variations in persister clones.
APOBEC3A knock‑down eliminates therapy‑induced APOBEC mutations, reduces double‑strand DNA breaks, and abolishes outgrowth of resistant persister populations in vitro and in vivo.
Patient tumor sequencing reveals enrichment of APOBEC signatures in resistant lesions that were absent pre‑treatment, supporting clinical relevance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: APOBEC‑mediated mutagenesis creates a mutational reservoir that enables late‑emerging resistance mutations, representing an adaptive mutability mechanism distinct from pre‑existing resistant clones.
open_questions: Can pharmacologic inhibition of APOBEC3A improve outcomes when combined with EGFR/ALK TKIs in patients?
What determines which persister cells adopt the APOBEC‑driven mutagenic program versus other resistance pathways?

session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_title: Therapeutic Strategies to Address Cancer Cell Heterogeneity and Plasticity

models.name: Patient‑derived melanoma organoids co‑cultured with CAFs

MOA_summary: Marine introduced a morphology‑based “Origami” platform that quantifies cell surface area and volume to map melanoma cell states. He showed that the trametinib + palbociclib (TP) combination drives a uniform adaptive trajectory toward high‑volume, lysosome‑rich cells; these become vulnerable to nilotinib‑induced ferroptosis. Parallel high‑surface cells can be sensitized by ER‑Golgi inhibitors (e.g., brefeldin A). The quadruple TP + nilotinib + brefeldin A (TPNB) regimen eradicates heterogeneous persister populations in vitro and yields curative responses in mouse models, also enhancing anti‑PD‑1 efficacy via immunogenic cell death.
key_findings: Morphometric profiling (cell surface vs volume) distinguishes four melanoma transcriptional states without omics.
TP (trametinib + palbociclib) induces a uniform high‑volume, lysosome‑rich persister phenotype across 20 cell lines.
High‑volume persisters are selectively killed by nilotinib, a ferroptosis‑sensitizing agent; effect is rescued by ferroptosis inhibitors.
High‑surface persisters are sensitized by ER‑Golgi inhibitors (e.g., brefeldin A), which reduce surface area and enhance TP efficacy.
The quadruple combination TP + nilotinib + brefeldin A (TPNB) eliminates persisters in vitro and produces >1‑year disease control, with curative outcomes in some mouse models.
TPNB also converts refractory tumors to anti‑PD‑1 responders by inducing immunogenic cell death (HMGB1 release, antigen presentation).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Single‑agent BRAF/MEK inhibition selects for diverse persister phenotypes; uniform TP steering reduces heterogeneity but does not fully eradicate all resistant subpopulations.

combination_strategies.rationale: CDK4/6 inhibition synergizes with MEK inhibition to drive uniform adaptive state
Exploits lysosome/iron dependence of high‑volume persisters to induce ferroptosis
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced cell death of high‑volume persisters

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduces cell surface area of high‑surface persisters, sensitizing them to TP
combination_strategies.phenotype: decreased surface area, increased TP efficacy

combination_strategies.rationale: TPNB induces immunogenic cell death, enhancing checkpoint blockade response
combination_strategies.phenotype: tumor regression and immune infiltration in refractory models

open_questions: Can FDA‑approved analogs of brefeldin A replace it for clinical translation?
What biomarkers predict which patients will benefit from TP‑based steering in the clinic?

session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_title: The Hidden Lives of Cancer Cells: Somatic Evolution, Cellular Plasticity, and the Detection of Residual Disease

speaker.name: Dan Landau
speaker.affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine and New York Genome Center

models.name: Smart PTA 3,500‑cell esophageal normal epithelium dataset

MOA_summary: Landau presented a phylogenetic framework that reconstructs high‑resolution tumor trees from single‑cell methylome or whole‑genome data, enabling measurement of heritability versus plasticity of cell states. By annotating leaves with transcriptomic phenotypes, he showed that stem‑like, astrocytic, and mesenchymal states in glioblastoma are heritable, while transition probabilities reveal lineage‑specific intermediates. Parallelly, he described whole‑genome ctDNA analysis using AI‑enhanced error suppression and ultra‑deep sequencing to quantify tumor burden (parts‑per‑million) and detect mutational signatures (APOBEC, tobacco) without tumor‑informed panels, providing early MRD detection and treatment monitoring.
key_findings: Methylation‑derived epimutations enable reconstruction of patient‑specific phylogenies from a single tumor sample.
Stem‑like, astrocytic, and mesenchymal GBM states are heritable across cell divisions, as shown by high Moran's I values.
Transition probability analysis reveals a required astrocytic intermediate for stem‑to‑mesenchymal conversion.
In IDH‑mutant gliomas, differentiation follows a unidirectional hierarchy; IDH‑wt GBM shows bidirectional plasticity with dedifferentiation.
Smart PTA scaling to 3,500 cells allows tracing normal esophageal clones back to conception, revealing long‑lived TP53‑mutant clones without pathology.
Whole‑genome ctDNA sequencing with AI error suppression achieves ppm‑level sensitivity, enabling detection of tumor‑derived mutational signatures (APOBEC, tobacco) without a tumor‑informed assay.
Early ctDNA dynamics predict immunotherapy response; steroid use blunts ctDNA‑driven benefit, highlighting clinical utility.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Bidirectional plasticity in IDH‑wt GBM suggests that mature cells can dedifferentiate, challenging stem‑cell‑targeted strategies.
open_questions: Can phylogenetic plasticity metrics be integrated into prospective MRD monitoring to predict therapeutic resistance?
What is the minimal number of single‑cell genomes required for robust phylogenetic inference in clinical samples?

session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_title: MRD Directed Treatment Strategies in Colorectal Cancer

biomarkers.name: ctDNA
biomarkers.assay: tumor‑informed personalized NGS panel / whole‑genome sequencing
biomarkers.finding: Post‑operative ctDNA positivity predicts near‑certain recurrence across stages

biomarkers.name: ctDNA molecular burden (mutant molecules/mL)
biomarkers.finding: Higher burden (>0.38 mut/mL) associates with worse 3‑yr RFS compared to lower burden

models.name: ALTTEAR (TAS‑102)
models.genotype_or_key_features: ctDNA‑positive post‑op patients receiving TAS‑102 vs placebo

clinical_data.metric: ctDNA clearance at end of treatment

clinical_data.value: Converged by 18 months, no OS benefit

MOA_summary: Tee reviewed how postoperative ctDNA serves as a highly sensitive MRD biomarker in colorectal cancer, stratifying patients into high‑risk (ctDNA‑positive) and low‑risk (ctDNA‑negative) groups. ctDNA‑guided trials (DYNAMIC, DYNAMIC‑3) demonstrated that chemotherapy can be safely omitted in ctDNA‑negative stage II patients without compromising 5‑yr RFS, and that ctDNA‑positive patients exhibit heterogeneous outcomes based on molecular burden. Persistent ctDNA after adjuvant therapy predicts poor RFS, highlighting a need for escalated or novel therapies, while ctDNA clearance may serve as an early surrogate endpoint for regulatory approval.
key_findings: Post‑operative ctDNA positivity predicts near‑certain recurrence across all CRC stages.
In DYNAMIC (stage II), ctDNA‑guided management halved chemotherapy use (28%→15%) with non‑inferior 5‑yr RFS (88.3% vs 87.2%).
In DYNAMIC‑3 (stage III), ctDNA‑negative de‑escalation reduced oxaliplatin use (89%→35%) but missed the non‑inferiority margin (−2.8% difference, CI crossed −7.5%).
Higher ctDNA molecular burden (>0.38 mut/mL) identifies a subgroup with 3‑yr RFS of only 23% versus 77% for low burden.
Persistent ctDNA after adjuvant therapy correlates with 3‑yr RFS of 14% versus 79% for cleared patients, suggesting ctDNA clearance as a surrogate efficacy marker.
ALTTEAR trial (TAS‑102) showed no survival benefit despite modest ctDNA clearance, indicating cytotoxic rechallenge alone may be insufficient for ctDNA‑positive disease.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ctDNA‑positive patients with high molecular burden remain at high risk despite intensified chemotherapy, implying intrinsic resistance mechanisms beyond standard oxaliplatin regimens.

combination_strategies.phenotype: No significant improvement in DFS or ctDNA clearance

combination_strategies.rationale: Induce immune response against residual clones

combination_strategies.with_agent: Novel immunotherapy combinations (e.g., anti‑PD‑1 + novel agents)
combination_strategies.phenotype: Multiple phase II/III platform trials underway

open_questions: What is the optimal ctDNA assay sensitivity (e.g., ppm vs ppb) needed to reliably guide de‑escalation without missing residual disease?
Which therapeutic modalities (e.g., targeted agents, immunotherapy, ADCs) can overcome the poor prognosis of high‑burden ctDNA‑positive patients?
